title: Humble Dependence in Times of Discouragement
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Humble Dependence in Times of Discouragement - Pastor Tito Lyro - Psalm 119_17-24.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Discouragement of the Psalmist
subpoints: The Gimmel section of Psalm 119 is marked by a sadness and awareness of trials — Derek Kidner labeled it 'Salus and Loneliness'
The Psalmist feels near death (v.17), out of place in the world (v.19), crushed with longing (v.20), under reproach and contempt (v.22), and falsely accused by ruling authorities (v.23)
All of this comes because he is faithful to the Word of God — life is not smooth even for the obedient
Theme: humility and dependence in the midst of discouragement

outline.title: 1. The Psalmist's Humble Response to Discouragement
subpoints: In discouragement he leans into God rather than pulling away — he holds on tightly to God and depends on him
Verse 17: he does not ask God to recognize his obedience; he asks God to deal with him 'bountifully' — beyond any measure of deserving; he throws himself upon God's grace
He addresses himself as God's servant (vv.17, 23); in the Septuagint the word is doulos — a slave purchased at a price; this is Paul's favorite self-designation
Verse 21: 'You rebuke the proud, the cursed, who stray from your commandments' — the proud is the opposite of the servant; the proud is the cursed while the humble is the blessed

outline.title: 2. The Psalmist's Dependence on God for Life Itself
subpoints: Verse 17: 'Deal bountifully with your servant that I may live' — apart from God's grace, he cannot continue living; he depends on the Lord for life itself
Jesus says: 'I have come that they may have life and have it more abundantly'; 'I am the bread of life'; 'I am the resurrection and the life'
The question Jesus asks Martha is asked to each of us: 'Do you believe this?' — is Jesus Christ your life?
Are you depending on God for life itself as the Psalmist did, or are you trying to sustain yourself?

outline.title: 3. The Psalmist's Dependence on God to Understand the Word
subpoints: Verse 18: 'Open my eyes that I may see wonderful things from your law' — no one can truly see the wonders of God's Word unless God illuminates
2 Corinthians 3:14-18: the Jews read Moses with a veil over their eyes; those in Christ have the veil removed and are being transformed from glory to glory
The wonderful things are already there — we pray that God would open our eyes to see what is present; come to the Bible with expectation
Verse 19: 'Do not hide your commandments from me' — he was terrified not of lacking a copy of the Bible, but of failing to understand it; the thought crushed his soul (v.20)

outline.title: 4. Finding Solace in the Word Under Pressure
subpoints: Verse 22: he relies on God to vindicate him — 'Remove from me reproach and contempt'; he does not need to worry about false charges; God will vindicate in this life or the life to come
Verse 23: 'Princes also sit and speak against me, but your servant meditates on your statutes' — rulers declaring evil against him in the place of public judgment
Spurgeon: 'The best answer to accusing princes is the word of a justifying King' — when falsely accused, hear the words of the King who justified you in Christ
Verse 24: 'Your testimonies also are my delight and my counselors' — the Word brings delight and counsel in the darkest times

outline.title: Conclusion: Three Applications
subpoints: Life in this world is hard because this is not our home — the Psalmist calls himself 'a stranger in the earth'; the more we grow in Christ, the more out of place we feel; we are time travelers from the age to come
Unbelievers cannot understand why we do not run to what the world offers; our deep differences mean that relationships with unbelievers rarely go deeper than an inch

The Psalmist is consumed with knowing and being the Word of God: 'My soul breaks with longing for your judgments at all times' — this must be every Christian's testimony; Philippians 3:7-11 describes Paul pursuing Christ as his consuming desire

summary:
Preaching from Psalm 119:17-24 — the Gimmel stanza — Pastor Tito Lyro examines the Psalmist's experience of deep discouragement: he feels near death, alienated in the world, crushed with longing for God, under reproach, and falsely accused by civil rulers. All of this presses down on him precisely because he is faithful to the Word of God. The sermon's theme — drawn directly from the text — is humility and dependence in the midst of discouragement.

The Psalmist's response is striking: he does not pull away from God but leans in harder. In verse 17 he asks not for what he deserves but for God to deal 'bountifully' with him — beyond any measure of merit. He addresses himself as God's doulos, the slave purchased at a price, which was also the Apostle Paul's favorite self-description. He depends on God for life itself (v.17), for illumination of the Word (v.18), for vindication against enemies (v.22), and for comfort under the accusations of rulers (v.23).

Three closing applications: first, life in this world is hard because believers are not citizens of it — we are time travelers from the age to come, living in this age according to the next, which creates inevitable friction. Second, the Psalmist's utter absorption in the Word of God — 'My soul breaks with longing for your judgments' — should be the testimony of every Christian. Third, discouragement rightly handled produces humility, which in turn opens us to the care of our brothers and sisters, to casting our anxieties on God (1 Peter 5:6-7), and to thinking more of others than of ourselves.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Discouragement of the Psalmist

We continue our series through Psalm 119, turning to the Gimmel section — verse 17. This letter originally represented a camel. Psalm 119, verse 17, reading through verse 24:

*Deal bountifully with your servant, that I may live and keep your word. Open my eyes, that I may see wondrous things from your law. I am a stranger in the earth; do not hide your commandments from me. My soul breaks with longing for your judgments at all times. You rebuke the proud, the cursed, who stray from your commandments. Remove from me reproach and contempt, for I have kept your testimonies. Princes also sit and speak against me, but your servant meditates on your statutes. Your testimonies also are my delight and my counselors.*

The more I read this Psalm, the more convicting it becomes to me. I do not have this order. I do not have this fervor. I do not have this zeal that the Psalmist has for the Word of God and the God of the Word. So I find my heart often cold to the Word of God — I find myself reading it and not really living it or even taking it in. I am praying that as we go through this Psalm over the next couple of months, our hearts will grow warmer to the Word of God.

There is a certain sadness and discouragement that comes across in this stanza. The Psalmist is more aware of the trials of life. If you follow the Psalm from the beginning, what gave birth to this Psalm was suffering. In the Gimmel stanza and the following Dalet section, that sadness is more present. Here we find discouragement because of circumstances. Derek Kidner, the Old Testament scholar, labeled this section 'Salus and Loneliness' — the Psalmist is looking for solace in his loneliness.

The theme is: humility and dependence in the midst of discouragement.

## The Psalmist's Discouragement

We can see that he is discouraged. In verse 17, he does not think he can keep on living — 'Deal bountifully with your servant, that I may live and keep your word.' On his own, he cannot go on. He needed God to help him.

In verse 19, the Psalmist feels out of place in the world: 'I am a stranger in the earth.' He felt God was distant from him — and prayed that God would not hide his commandments from him.

In verse 20, his soul was broken or crushed with longing for the judgments of God. You long for something that is far away, something you are missing. He felt God was distant.

He was also experiencing reproach and contempt for his obedience to the Lord — verse 22: 'Remove from me reproach and contempt, for I have kept your testimonies.' We all face reproach and contempt at times. But sometimes it just grinds us down. It gets tiring to swim against the flow the entire time, to fight against everything going on around you — and that can be very discouraging.

He also had powerful people saying evil and untrue things about him — verse 23: 'Princes also sit and speak against me.' The idea of sitting here is sitting in the place of public judgment. These are rulers sitting in official judgment and declaring evil and untrue things about the Psalmist, leading the whole community to speak evil against him.

All of these discouraging things were happening because he was faithful to the Word of God and the God of the Word. Life was not going smoothly even though he was faithful.

Times of discouragement tend to drive us away from God — we pull away from him, from his Word, from his people, we stop praying. Discouragement can be lethal to our souls. But this man, by God's good grace, resisted the temptation to pull away — and instead leaned into God. In his discouragement he runs to God, leans into God, and holds on tightly to him. He responds in humility, depending on God.

## 1. Humility in Discouragement

You see his humility in verse 17, where he prays that God will deal with him according to grace. He says, 'Lord, deal with me bountifully, your servant.' He is not asking for what he deserves. He is not saying, 'Lord, look how good I was; look at all the ways I obeyed you.' He recognizes that his obedience is worth nothing — he is asking God to deal with him beyond any measure of deserving, according to pure grace.

In his humility he knows he is only a servant of the Lord. He could have addressed God as Father. He could have said he was a covenant member of God's people. And yet he addresses God as God's servant — verse 17 and again in verse 23: 'But your servant meditates on your statutes.' This word 'servant' is interesting: in the ancient Greek translation of the Old Testament — the Septuagint — the word used is doulos: a slave, a bond-servant purchased at a price, a servant who had no rights, no inheritance. And that is how the Apostle Paul most often identified himself.

Are we not all slaves purchased at a price? And what was the price? The very blood of the Lord Jesus Christ. That is why Paul says in 1 Corinthians 6: 'For you were bought at a price; therefore glorify God in your body and in your spirit, which are God's.'

So he approaches God as a servant — one who does not deserve anything from God. He knows he cannot understand the Scriptures apart from God's help. Verse 18: 'Open my eyes that I may see wonderful things from your law.' I cannot do it on my own. In his humility he understands that one must approach the Word of God with a humble heart.

In verse 21: 'You rebuke the proud, the cursed, who stray from your commandments.' He understood that the Lord rebukes the proud and the arrogant. According to this verse, the proud is the one who strays from the commands of God — the proud is the opposite of the servant. The proud is cursed while the humble is blessed.

Isaiah 57:15 says: 'For thus says the high and lofty One who inhabits eternity, whose name is Holy: I dwell in the high and holy place with him who has a contrite and humble spirit, to revive the spirit of the humble and to revive the heart of the contrite ones.' The exalted, high, lofty, eternal, holy God is not looking for the mighty, the proud, or the sure of themselves. He is looking for the humble — those with a contrite and humble spirit — to be associated with him.

Humility is needed in discouragement because it lets us see that we need someone other than ourselves to pull us out. If we are proud and discouraged, we will just remain discouraged. Humility allows us to cast our anxieties upon the Lord — because we know he cares for us (1 Peter 5:6-7). Humility allows us to open ourselves to our brothers and sisters to help us see if there is any sin that is deceiving us — as Hebrews 3:12-13 tells us. Humility allows us to consider others more significant than ourselves, looking at their interests instead of our own (Philippians 2:3-4) — and one way to get out of discouragement is to start thinking of how we can serve other people.

## 2. Dependence on God for Life Itself

In his discouragement, the Psalmist relies on God. In verse 17, he recognizes that he depends on God for life itself: 'Deal bountifully with your servant, that I may live.' Apart from God dealing bountifully with him, he will not be able to live.

Are you there? Are you relying on God for life itself? Jesus says: 'I have come that they may have life and have it more abundantly.' He says: 'I am the way, the life, and the truth.' John says about Jesus: 'In him was life and the life was the light of men.' Jesus says about himself: 'For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have everlasting life.' He says: 'I am the bread of life.' He says: 'I am the resurrection and the life; he who believes in me, though he may die, he shall live, and whoever lives and believes in me shall never die.' And then Jesus asks Martha — and asks you — a crucial question: 'Do you believe this?'

Is Jesus Christ your life? Is he the one who has brought you back from spiritual death and will bring you back from physical death at his coming? Do you depend on God for life like the Psalmist did?

## 3. Dependence on God to Understand His Word

The Psalmist also depends on God in order to understand the Word of God. In verse 18: 'Open my eyes that I may see wonderful things from your law.' No one can really see the true wonders of God's Word unless God illuminates or enlightens us to see what is there.

Paul in 2 Corinthians 3 says that the Jews read Moses with a veil over their eyes and cannot understand it. But those who are in Christ have that veil removed — and because of that we are being transformed into the same image from glory to glory, just as the Spirit of the Lord does.

When God opens our eyes to see what is in his Word, it is like scales falling from our eyes, as happened to Saul after his encounter with the Lord Jesus on the road to Damascus. I started reading the Bible as an unbeliever. It made no sense to me; it was boring. I was a chemical engineer — a math guy, a science guy. And the moment the Lord changed my heart, the truth of creation from the Scriptures, the truth of how bad I am from Romans 3, my need for a Savior — it was not a progressive awakening. It was immediate. Like scales falling off.

I want you to notice from verse 18 that the wonderful things are already there. We are not praying that God would put them there — we are praying that he would let us see what is already present. Come to the Bible with the expectation of seeing wonderful things, because they are there.

And 'wonderful' in the literal sense: full of wonder. Not merely neat or interesting, but full of wonder. What is another word that means nearly the same thing but is rarely used in that sense today? 'Awful' — full of awe. These are the same kind of words. We read the Bible as God opens our eyes and we are awestruck by what is there.

The Psalmist cannot stand the idea of not understanding the wonderful things in the Word of God. Verse 19: 'Do not hide your commandments from me.' He is not afraid he will lose his copy of the Bible. The 'hiding' here is that God would withhold the meaning of the Word from him. He is terrified of not understanding the Word of God — and that terror breaks or crushes his soul. Verse 20: 'My soul breaks with longing for your judgments at all times.' He longs for the Word.

## 4. Dependence on God for Vindication and Comfort

In verse 22, as he comes to God in humility, he depends on God to vindicate him before his enemies: 'Remove from me reproach and contempt, for I have kept your testimonies.' He relies on God to deal with whatever has been brought against him. God will vindicate his people — if not in this life, in the life to come. We do not need to worry about false charges. We need to worry about true things said about us — not reproach or contempt, because God will vindicate his people.

And he depends on the Lord even in the most severe persecution. Verse 23: 'Princes also sit and speak against me, but your servant meditates on your statutes.' In times of pressure, we tend to forget the Word of God. The Psalmist says: in those times, I meditate on it even more. Rulers and princes — the civil magistrates, the leaders of the community — are speaking evil of him. He finds solace and comfort in meditating on the Word of God.

Spurgeon says: 'The best answer to accusing princes is the word of a justifying King.' When false accusations are brought against you and you are brought low, the best way to deal with it is by hearing the words of the King — not the little rulers of the community, but the King who justified you in Jesus Christ.

## Conclusion: Three Final Applications

**First:** one of the reasons life here is difficult is that this age, this world, is not our home. The Psalmist says in verse 19, 'I am a stranger in the earth.' Peter says the same: 'Beloved, I beg you as sojourners and pilgrims...' We live the life to come now, and that creates this dissonance with the world around us. Because of that, we are mocked, scorned, and looked upon with contempt (v.22). We are called unintelligent, called simple-tons for our commitment to God. But we are in good company — Matthew 27 describes what they did to our Lord himself.

Jonathan Leeman gives the illustration: we are all time travelers. Every Christian is a time traveler who has traveled from the age to come back to now, endeavoring to live according to the age to come. That creates all kinds of problems with the timeline — which is why life can be discouraging.

**Second:** the unbeliever cannot understand why we do not run to what the world has to offer. Paul says they think it strange that we do not run with them in the same flood of dissipation, speaking evil of us. We live in two different ages.

**Third:** the Psalmist is consumed with knowing and being the Word of God. Verse 20: 'My soul breaks with longing for your judgments at all times.' He understands that he was made to glorify and enjoy God — and this is not a fleeting desire but one that is present all the time. It is how every Christian should feel. Paul in Philippians 3:7-11 says: 'What things were gain to me, these I have counted loss for Christ... I also count all things loss for the excellence of the knowledge of Jesus Christ my Lord... that I may gain Christ and be found in him, not having my own righteousness which is from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the righteousness which is from God by faith.'

If that is not your desire where you are today — look to Christ. If your desire is not to know Christ more, if your desire is not to pursue him and count all things loss — examine yourself to see if you are truly in Christ.

Brothers and sisters, the Lord is so good to us. He has given us his Word so we can know him. This portion of Psalm 119 shows us that in times of discouragement, we respond in humility and dependence on the Lord — and by his grace he pulls us out of the miry pit, sets our feet upon a rock, and gives us a new song.

Father, you have done so much for us. Thank you that you have opened our eyes to see great things because we are in Christ. Help us to be awestruck by all your Word teaches us. Help us to pursue Christ with every ounce of our being. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
